Okay so there's also the idea of task vectors and basically task vectors here we are just merging together to models by taking the parameters of the models and averaging them together task vectors and other related works specifically take advantage of the fact that we're looking at different fine tune models and so these are models where we have a base model and we know that.

That we fine tuned from this base model and the basic idea is that we have our base model here and the task vector is the difference between the base models vector parameters and the fine tune models parameters so that's what they define as a task vector.

What does this allow us to do this allows us to do a number of interesting things the first one is that we can actually subtract out a quote unquote tasks that we don't want so like let's say we had a model that was trained on lots of toxic text or we had a model that was trained on lots of private text or something like that we could actually.
So we actually subtract out the task vector from this and basically attempt to remove the models ability to do that sort of things.
You can also take two task vectors and combine them together and like get the model from the combination of the two.

This isn't exactly the same is averaging the parameters because if you average the parameters you would probably get something in the middle right here but if you average the two vectors or add the two vectors together you get something over here actually sorry if you average the vectors may be the same.
So you could like add together the two vectors and that would be something different and taking the average so it gives you a little bit more flexibility about things to do.

And another thing this allows you to do is this allows you to try to result conflicts between the vectors of different tasks and so this is an illustration of this method here and this has three tasks basically it has model one model two model three.

And each of them has vectors and you'll see that in some cases these vectors can conflict so we have like pink going up we have yellow and purple going down we have yellow going up we have pink and purple going down etc etc and what this does is this identifies the vectors that are pointing the most strongly in particular directions and then it resolves conflicts between them and comes up with a vector that tries to move in a direction that improves all of the tasks.
Same time they demonstrate that this is the better method for kind of improving the ability to do all of the tasks compared to just the average things together.
